title: Enterprise Android & Managed Devices
description: Work profiles, managed configurations, DLP, compliance checks, and enterprise-specific Gemini considerations
lesson:
Work Profiles & Managed Devices

A work profile is a separate Android user profile that runs on the same physical device. It creates a sandboxed environment with its own app data, accounts, and encryption keys — while sharing the underlying hardware (screen, CPU, radios) with the personal profile.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PHYSICAL DEVICE │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ Personal Profile │ │ Work Profile │ │ │ │ │ │ (badge icon 🔒) │ │ │ │ Personal Gmail │ │ Corporate Gmail │ │ │ │ Personal Photos │ │ Managed Apps │ │ │ │ Personal Chrome │ │ Corporate Slack │ │ │ │ User's own apps │ │ IT-deployed apps │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Own data store │ │ Own data store │ │ │ │ Own contacts │ │ Own contacts │ │ │ │ Own clipboard* │ │ Own clipboard* │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ │ ↕ SHARED: hardware, kernel, display ↕ │ │ ✗ NOT shared: data, apps, credentials │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ * Clipboard sharing is controlled by IT policy

Device Owner vs Profile Owner

Enterprise management has two modes, depending on whether IT owns the entire device or just a work profile on an employee's personal device:

Device Owner (DO)Profile Owner (PO)
ScopeFull device controlWork profile only
Use caseCompany-owned device (kiosk, warehouse scanner)BYOD — employee's personal phone
Can wipe?Yes — entire deviceYes — work profile only
Camera controlCan disable device-wideCan disable in work profile only
Network configCan set Wi-Fi, VPN for whole deviceCan set per-app VPN for work apps
Set viaDeviceAdminReceiver during initial setup (NFC, QR)ACTION_PROVISION_MANAGED_PROFILE

DevicePolicyManager Key APIs

// DevicePolicyManager — the central API for enterprise control val dpm = getSystemService(Context.DEVICE_POLICY_SERVICE) as DevicePolicyManager val adminComponent = ComponentName(this, MyDeviceAdminReceiver::class.java) // Disable the camera (Device Owner: device-wide, Profile Owner: work profile) dpm.setCameraDisabled(adminComponent, true) // Require a minimum password quality dpm.setPasswordQuality(adminComponent, DevicePolicyManager.PASSWORD_QUALITY_ALPHANUMERIC) dpm.setPasswordMinimumLength(adminComponent, 8) // Remote wipe — erases data (DO: whole device, PO: work profile only) dpm.wipeData(0) // flags: WIPE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE, WIPE_RESET_PROTECTION_DATA // Lock the device immediately dpm.lockNow() // Set maximum failed password attempts before wipe dpm.setMaximumFailedPasswordsForWipe(adminComponent, 10)

Cross-Profile Intent Communication

By default, intents cannot cross the profile boundary. IT admins (via the DPC) must explicitly whitelist which intents can flow between personal and work profiles:

// IT admin allows web links to be opened across profiles val filter = IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_VIEW) filter.addCategory(Intent.CATEGORY_BROWSABLE) filter.addDataScheme("https") // Allow intents FROM managed profile TO parent (personal) dpm.addCrossProfileIntentFilter( adminComponent, filter, DevicePolicyManager.FLAG_MANAGED_CAN_ACCESS_PARENT // work → personal ) // Allow intents FROM parent TO managed profile dpm.addCrossProfileIntentFilter( adminComponent, filter, DevicePolicyManager.FLAG_PARENT_CAN_ACCESS_MANAGED // personal → work ) // Remove all cross-profile intent filters dpm.clearCrossProfileIntentFilters(adminComponent)
Content sharing restrictions: By default, copy-paste, file sharing, and contact lookup across profiles are blocked. IT can selectively enable them via DevicePolicyManager.addCrossProfileIntentFilter() and user restrictions like DISALLOW_CROSS_PROFILE_COPY_PASTE.

Managed Google Play & App Distribution

An EMM (Enterprise Mobility Management) system is the IT admin console that manages devices, apps, and policies. Examples: Google Workspace MDM, VMware Workspace ONE, Microsoft Intune. The EMM communicates with devices through Google's Android Management API.

┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ IT Admin │────▶│ EMM Console │────▶│ Google Play EMM │ │ (web UI) │ │ (Intune, WS ONE) │ │ API │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ └────────┬─────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Managed Device │ │ │ │ • Install apps │ │ • Push configs │ │ • Enforce policy │ │ • Wipe remotely │ └──────────────────┘

Managed Configurations

IT admins can push key-value configuration to apps without modifying the app's code. The app declares what configuration it accepts, and the EMM pushes values at deploy time.

// 1. Declare managed configs in res/xml/app_restrictions.xml <restrictions xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"> <restriction android:key="server_url" android:title="Server URL" android:restrictionType="string" android:defaultValue="https://api.example.com" /> <restriction android:key="enable_analytics" android:title="Enable Analytics" android:restrictionType="bool" android:defaultValue="true" /> <restriction android:key="max_cache_mb" android:title="Max Cache Size (MB)" android:restrictionType="integer" android:defaultValue="100" /> </restrictions> // 2. Reference in AndroidManifest.xml <application ...> <meta-data android:name="android.content.APP_RESTRICTIONS" android:resource="@xml/app_restrictions" /> </application>

Reading Managed Config with RestrictionsManager

// Read managed configuration pushed by the IT admin val restrictionsManager = getSystemService(Context.RESTRICTIONS_SERVICE) as RestrictionsManager val appRestrictions: Bundle = restrictionsManager.applicationRestrictions val serverUrl = appRestrictions.getString("server_url", "https://api.example.com") val analyticsEnabled = appRestrictions.getBoolean("enable_analytics", true) val maxCacheMb = appRestrictions.getInt("max_cache_mb", 100) // Listen for configuration changes at runtime val restrictionsFilter = IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_APPLICATION_RESTRICTIONS_CHANGED) registerReceiver(object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { val newRestrictions = restrictionsManager.applicationRestrictions // Re-read and apply updated config applyConfig(newRestrictions) } }, restrictionsFilter)

Silent Installation & Private Apps

Silent install: EMMs with Device Owner privileges can install and update apps without user interaction via PackageInstaller sessions. The user sees no prompt — the app appears in the work profile automatically.

Private apps: Organizations can publish apps to Managed Google Play that are visible only to their organization's devices. These never appear in the public Play Store. IT admins can also host private apps directly (no Play Store upload) via managed Google Play iframes.

Data Loss Prevention (DLP)

DLP is critical in enterprise environments where sensitive corporate data must not leak to personal apps or external services.

Preventing Copy-Paste Leakage

// Approach 1: Disable cross-profile copy-paste via DevicePolicyManager dpm.addUserRestriction(adminComponent, UserManager.DISALLOW_CROSS_PROFILE_COPY_PASTE) // Users cannot copy text from work apps and paste into personal apps // Approach 2: App-level clipboard restriction // Detect and clear clipboard if it contains sensitive data val clipboardManager = getSystemService(Context.CLIPBOARD_SERVICE) as ClipboardManager // Clear clipboard when app goes to background (onStop) override fun onStop() { super.onStop() if (isSensitiveScreen) { clipboardManager.setPrimaryClip(ClipData.newPlainText("", "")) } }

Screenshot & Screen Recording Blocking

// FLAG_SECURE prevents screenshots AND screen recording class SecureActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) // Must be set BEFORE setContentView window.setFlags( WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE, WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE ) setContentView(R.layout.activity_secure) } } // In Compose — set FLAG_SECURE on the Activity's window @Composable fun SecureScreen() { val context = LocalContext.current DisposableEffect(Unit) { val window = (context as Activity).window window.setFlags( WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE, WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE ) onDispose { window.clearFlags(WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE) } } // Screen content here — cannot be captured }
What FLAG_SECURE actually does:
1. Screenshots via power+volume: shows a black/blank image
2. Screen recording: the secured window appears black in the recording
3. Recent apps thumbnail: shows a blank preview
4. Cast/mirror: the secured window is not visible on the external display
5. Accessibility services: still have access to the content (accessibility takes precedence)

Content Sharing Restrictions

// Prevent sharing data INTO the managed (work) profile dpm.addUserRestriction( adminComponent, UserManager.DISALLOW_SHARE_INTO_MANAGED_PROFILE ) // Prevent sharing data OUT of the managed profile // (done by NOT adding cross-profile intent filters for ACTION_SEND) // Block Bluetooth sharing from work profile dpm.addUserRestriction(adminComponent, UserManager.DISALLOW_BLUETOOTH_SHARING) // Block USB file transfer dpm.addUserRestriction(adminComponent, UserManager.DISALLOW_USB_FILE_TRANSFER)

Network DLP

Network-level DLP ensures sensitive data (including AI/LLM responses) does not leave the managed network:
1. Per-app VPN: Route all work app traffic through a corporate VPN (see Section 4)
2. Certificate pinning: Prevent MITM inspection by pinning to corporate CA certificates
3. Proxy configuration: Force all HTTP traffic through a corporate proxy that inspects and filters content
4. DNS filtering: Block access to unapproved cloud AI services from work profile apps

App Configuration & Compliance

Reading Configuration from EMM

// Complete pattern: read EMM config on startup + listen for changes class EnterpriseConfigManager(private val context: Context) { private val restrictionsManager = context.getSystemService(Context.RESTRICTIONS_SERVICE) as RestrictionsManager data class EnterpriseConfig( val apiEndpoint: String, val enableOfflineMode: Boolean, val maxTokenLimit: Int, val allowCloudProcessing: Boolean ) fun readConfig(): EnterpriseConfig { val restrictions = restrictionsManager.applicationRestrictions return EnterpriseConfig( apiEndpoint = restrictions.getString("api_endpoint", "https://api.corp.example.com"), enableOfflineMode = restrictions.getBoolean("enable_offline_mode", false), maxTokenLimit = restrictions.getInt("max_token_limit", 4096), allowCloudProcessing = restrictions.getBoolean("allow_cloud_processing", true) ) } }

Compliance Checks

// Check device compliance before allowing access to corporate data fun checkDeviceCompliance(context: Context): ComplianceResult { val dpm = context.getSystemService(Context.DEVICE_POLICY_SERVICE) as DevicePolicyManager val keyguardManager = context.getSystemService(Context.KEYGUARD_SERVICE) as KeyguardManager val issues = mutableListOf<String>() // Is storage encrypted? val encryptionStatus = dpm.storageEncryptionStatus if (encryptionStatus != DevicePolicyManager.ENCRYPTION_STATUS_ACTIVE && encryptionStatus != DevicePolicyManager.ENCRYPTION_STATUS_ACTIVE_PER_USER) { issues.add("Device storage is not encrypted") } // Is a screen lock set? if (!keyguardManager.isDeviceSecure) { issues.add("No screen lock configured") } // Is the device rooted? (basic check) val suPaths = listOf("/system/bin/su", "/system/xbin/su", "/sbin/su") if (suPaths.any { File(it).exists() }) { issues.add("Device may be rooted") } return if (issues.isEmpty()) ComplianceResult.Compliant else ComplianceResult.NonCompliant(issues) }

Play Integrity API (Successor to SafetyNet)

// Play Integrity API — verify the device is genuine and uncompromised val integrityManager = IntegrityManagerFactory.create(applicationContext) // 1. Request an integrity token (nonce should come from YOUR server) val request = IntegrityTokenRequest.builder() .setNonce(nonceFromServer) // Base64-encoded server nonce for replay protection .build() integrityManager.requestIntegrityToken(request) .addOnSuccessListener { response -> val token = response.token() // 2. Send token to YOUR server for verification sendTokenToBackend(token) } .addOnFailureListener { e -> // Handle error — device may be unverifiable } // 3. On your server: decrypt token → get verdict // Verdict includes: // - deviceIntegrity: MEETS_DEVICE_INTEGRITY (not rooted, genuine) // - appIntegrity: PLAY_RECOGNIZED (installed from Play Store) // - accountDetails: LICENSED (user has a Play license)
Never verify integrity tokens on-device. The token must be sent to your backend server and verified there using Google's Integrity API. On-device verification can be bypassed by a rooted device — the whole point is that you don't trust the device.

Per-App VPN

// Force all traffic from work profile through a corporate VPN val dpm = getSystemService(Context.DEVICE_POLICY_SERVICE) as DevicePolicyManager // Set always-on VPN — all network traffic goes through this VPN app dpm.setAlwaysOnVpnPackage( adminComponent, "com.corp.vpn", // Package name of the VPN app true // lockdownEnabled: block all traffic if VPN is disconnected ) // With lockdown = true: // - If VPN disconnects → ALL network access is blocked // - No data can leak through an unprotected connection // - Users cannot disable the VPN

Enterprise-Specific Gemini Considerations

When building an AI assistant in a managed enterprise environment, there are unique constraints beyond standard Android development.

Data Residency & Content Filtering

Key concerns for enterprise AI:
1. Data residency: AI responses containing corporate data must stay within approved geographic regions. The managed config should specify which API endpoint to use (e.g., EU-only servers).
2. Content filtering: Enterprise AI must filter responses for PII, confidential project names, and regulated data before displaying to the user or logging.
3. Prompt injection: In a corporate context, prompt injection could leak internal documents — enterprise apps must sanitize inputs and validate outputs.

On-Device vs Cloud Processing

Decision: On-Device vs Cloud AI Processing ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Is data classified as "Highly Confidential"? ├── YES → On-device processing ONLY │ (Gemini Nano, ML Kit, TFLite) │ • No data leaves the device │ • Limited model capability │ • Works offline │ └── NO → Cloud processing allowed? (check managed config: allow_cloud_processing) ├── YES → Route through corporate proxy │ • Use per-app VPN │ • Log all queries for audit │ • Respect data residency region │ └── NO → On-device only • Same as highly confidential path

Token Management in Work Profiles

// Work profile has SEPARATE credential storage from personal profile // Each profile has its own AndroidKeyStore // Store API tokens in the work profile's KeyStore fun storeApiToken(token: String) { val keyStore = KeyStore.getInstance("AndroidKeyStore") keyStore.load(null) // Generate a key that is bound to the work profile val keyGenerator = KeyGenerator.getInstance( KeyProperties.KEY_ALGORITHM_AES, "AndroidKeyStore" ) keyGenerator.init( KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder("ai_api_key", KeyProperties.PURPOSE_ENCRYPT or KeyProperties.PURPOSE_DECRYPT) .setBlockModes(KeyProperties.BLOCK_MODE_GCM) .setEncryptionPaddings(KeyProperties.ENCRYPTION_PADDING_NONE) .setUserAuthenticationRequired(true) // Require unlock to use .build() ) // Key is automatically scoped to the current user/profile // Cannot be accessed from the personal profile } // If work profile is wiped, all keys in its KeyStore are destroyed // Personal profile keys remain intact

Audit Logging

// Enterprise AI apps should log all AI interactions for compliance data class AuditEntry( val timestamp: Long, val userId: String, val action: String, // "query", "response", "tool_use" val promptHash: String, // Hash of prompt (not raw text for privacy) val responseHash: String, val modelVersion: String, val processingLocation: String, // "on_device" or "cloud:us-east-1" val dataSensitivity: String // "public", "internal", "confidential" ) // Log to a tamper-resistant store (not SharedPreferences!) class AuditLogger(private val db: AuditDatabase) { suspend fun logInteraction(entry: AuditEntry) { db.auditDao().insert(entry) // Periodically sync to corporate SIEM via managed VPN if (db.auditDao().unsyncedCount() >= BATCH_SIZE) { syncToSiem() } } }

questions.title: Work Profile Architecture
questions.description: What is a work profile in Android enterprise?
options: A separate APK that runs in a container inside the main app
A separate Android user profile on the same device with its own sandboxed data, apps, and credentials
A VPN tunnel that encrypts all corporate app traffic
A special permission group that grants elevated privileges to work apps
questions.explanation: A work profile is a separate Android user profile that runs on the same physical device. It has its own app data directory, its own accounts, its own encryption keys, and its own set of installed apps (badged with a briefcase icon). The personal profile and work profile share hardware but are otherwise isolated — apps in one profile cannot directly access data in the other.
hints: Think about user-level isolation, not app-level isolation
Both profiles share the same hardware and kernel

questions.title: FLAG_SECURE Behavior
questions.description: An Activity sets FLAG_SECURE on its window. Which of the following is TRUE?
options: The Activity cannot be launched from an implicit intent
Screenshots show a black screen, screen recordings show black for that window, and the recent apps thumbnail is blank — but accessibility services can still read the content
The Activity is encrypted at rest and requires biometric authentication to open
The Activity cannot run in a work profile
questions.explanation: FLAG_SECURE prevents visual capture of the window contents: screenshots are blank, screen recordings show black for the secured window, and the recent apps overview shows a blank thumbnail. However, accessibility services still have access to the content tree — FLAG_SECURE is about visual capture, not programmatic access to the view hierarchy. Also, screen mirroring/casting will not show the secured window.
hints: Think about what FLAG_SECURE blocks: visual capture methods
There is one category of system service that still gets access for usability reasons

questions.title: Managed Configuration Reading
questions.description: What does this code do, and when would the BroadcastReceiver fire?
options: It reads user preferences from SharedPreferences and listens for preference changes
It reads managed configuration pushed by an IT admin via EMM and listens for runtime config updates
It reads Android system settings and watches for Settings changes
It reads build config values and listens for app updates
questions.explanation: RestrictionsManager.applicationRestrictions reads the managed configuration that an IT admin pushes through an EMM (Enterprise Mobility Management) console. The ACTION_APPLICATION_RESTRICTIONS_CHANGED broadcast fires whenever the IT admin updates the app's configuration remotely — allowing the app to re-read and apply new settings without a reinstall. This is the standard pattern for enterprise app configuration.
hints: RestrictionsManager is an enterprise API, not a user-facing preferences API
Who controls these values — the user or the IT admin?

questions.title: DLP: Preventing Data Leakage
questions.description: This enterprise chat screen displays sensitive corporate data but has no DLP protections. Add screenshot blocking and clipboard clearing when the user leaves the screen.

checks.message: Use FLAG_SECURE to prevent screenshots and screen recording
Clear the clipboard to prevent data leakage when leaving the screen

questions.explanation: FLAG_SECURE must be set before setContentView() to ensure the window is secured from the start. It prevents screenshots, screen recording, and recent apps thumbnails from capturing the content. Clearing the clipboard in onStop() ensures that if a user copied sensitive text, it won't be available when they switch to a personal app.
hints: FLAG_SECURE should be set before the content view is inflated
The clipboard is shared across the device — clear it when leaving a sensitive screen

questions.title: Play Integrity Verification
questions.description: Where should you verify a Play Integrity API token?
options: On the device, using the Play Integrity SDK's built-in verification method
On your backend server — the token must be sent to your server and verified there via Google's API
In the EMM console by uploading the token
Verification is automatic — the SDK handles it and returns a boolean
questions.explanation: Integrity tokens must always be verified on your backend server, never on-device. The purpose of the Play Integrity API is to verify that the device is genuine and the app is unmodified. If you verified the token on the device itself, a rooted/compromised device could simply fake the verification result. The flow is: device requests token → sends token to your server → your server calls Google's API to decrypt and verify → your server decides whether to trust the device.
hints: If the device is compromised, can you trust anything it tells you about itself?
Think about where the trust boundary is

questions.title: Device Owner vs Profile Owner
questions.description: Explain the difference between Device Owner and Profile Owner in Android enterprise. When would you use each?
questions.answer: A Device Owner (DO) has full control over the entire device — it can enforce policies device-wide, disable hardware features, configure networking, and wipe the entire device. It is used for company-owned devices like kiosks, warehouse scanners, or dedicated work phones. A Profile Owner (PO) controls only the work profile on a personal device (BYOD). It can manage work apps, enforce password policies for the work profile, and wipe the work profile without affecting personal data. PO is used when employees bring their own phones and the company needs to separate corporate data from personal data.
questions.explanation: The distinction maps to device ownership: company-owned device = Device Owner (IT controls everything), employee-owned device = Profile Owner (IT controls only the work sandbox). Profile Owner is the more common scenario today since most enterprise deployments use BYOD. Device Owner is provisioned during initial device setup (factory reset), while Profile Owner can be added to an already-set-up personal device.
hints: Think about who owns the device — the company or the employee
What data should IT be able to wipe in each scenario?
